articles.full_text:
40°C and rising: Navi Mumbai in the grip of a harsh heatwave
Navi Mumbai: As May begins, Navi Mumbai is grappling with an intense and prolonged heatwave, with temperatures repeatedly touching 38°C to 40°C over the past month. High humidity levels, often ranging between 60% and 75%, have made conditions more oppressive, significantly increasing the “feels-like” temperature and raising serious health concerns among residents.
Since late March, the city has witnessed a steady spike in heat intensity. Unlike last year, when April temperatures mostly stayed around 35–36°C, this year has seen frequent peaks nearing 40°C. Even nights have offered little relief, with temperatures hovering between 25°C and 28°C. According to the India Meteorological Department, delayed sea breeze patterns and prevailing atmospheric conditions have contributed to the persistence of “hot and humid” weather across the Mumbai Metropolitan Region.
Medical experts warn that the combination of high heat and humidity is particularly dangerous as it disrupts the body’s natural cooling mechanism. Reduced evaporation of sweat can quickly lead to heat exhaustion and, in severe cases, heatstroke. Early symptoms such as nausea, dizziness, fatigue, and dry skin are being widely reported.
For many residents, especially those working outdoors, the heat is more than just discomfort—it is affecting daily life and livelihoods.“It’s becoming extremely difficult to even step out during peak hours,” said Sadhna Mishra, a resident of Seawoods. “I’m a student and also work part-time, so I don’t really have the option to stay indoors despite the heat. Even after taking precautions like wearing a cap, staying hydrated, and carrying ORS, there comes a point when I feel completely drained. The heat is exhausting and affects both my focus and health. With rising temperatures every year, it feels like stepping outside is becoming more of a challenge, especially for those of us who have to manage studies and work together.”
“It’s not just the heat, it’s the constant exhaustion that comes with travelling every day,” said another commuter Yash Ekawde, a medical student from Ghansoli who daily commutes to Vashi. “By the time I reach college, I already feel fatigued, and attending long lectures or practicals becomes even more challenging. The crowded transport, combined with the humidity, makes it mentally draining as well. As a medical student, staying alert is crucial, but the weather and daily commute often take a toll on both my energy and concentration.”
With forecasts indicating continued heatwave conditions, authorities are urging citizens to limit outdoor exposure, stay hydrated, and remain alert to symptoms, as the city navigates one of its harshest summer spells in recent years.
